title: Doping- and temperature-dependent electronic structure and spin dynamics in the Hubbard model on a square lattice within cluster perturbation theory
authors: V. I. Kuz'min, S. G. Ovchinnikov
abstract: The doping and temperature dependencies of the electronic structure and the dynamic spin susceptibility of the Hubbard model on a square lattice are studied within cluster perturbation theory (CPT). The most general features of both electronic and spin spectra qualitatively agree with the experimental data on cuprates. Generalized mean-field calculations of the electronic structure using static short-range magnetic correlations from CPT are also implemented and compared with the results of CPT, allowing us to discuss the role of short-range antiferromagnetism in the formation of the pseudogap.
summary: This work applies Cluster Perturbation Theory to the Hubbard model on a square lattice to map out the electronic and spin properties across different doping and temperature regimes. It successfully reproduces key features of the pseudogap state observed in cuprates, highlighting the critical role of short-range antiferromagnetic correlations in shaping the electronic spectrum. The study advances the theoretical understanding of strongly correlated electron systems near superconducting phases.
main_problem: The study investigates the doping and temperature dependencies of the electronic structure and dynamic spin susceptibility in the Hubbard model, focusing on the mysterious Pseudogap (PG) state observed in cuprate superconductors.
main_result: The calculations show that the electronic structure exhibits a nodal-antinodal dichotomy, and the PG signature is visible as a strong suppression of spectral weight, with short-range antiferromagnetism being crucial for this behavior.
method: The primary theoretical tool is Cluster Perturbation Theory (CPT), supplemented by a Generalized Mean-Field Approximation (GMFA) to analyze spectral functions and spin dynamics.
model_or_system: The system is modeled by the Hubbard Hamiltonian on a square lattice, incorporating nearest and next-nearest hopping terms and a local Coulomb repulsion U. The analysis covers varying doping levels and temperatures.
key_observables: Electronic spectral function A(k, omega), dynamic transverse spin susceptibility spectrum, and the degree of spectral weight suppression at the Fermi level.
important_parameters: Doping ($p$), Temperature ($T$), Coulomb repulsion ($U$), and hopping parameters ($t, t'$).
main_assumptions: The use of CPT and GMFA introduces approximations, notably that the GMFA alone is insufficient to capture the full frequency dependence of the PG drop, suggesting the need for dynamic correlation terms.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the electronic spectral function across different doping/temperature regimes, showing the nodal-antinodal dichotomy, and present the dynamic spin susceptibility spectra for specific doping levels.
paper_structure: The paper systematically analyzes the electronic and spin spectra using CPT, comparing results with experimental data (ARPES, INS). It progresses from general spectral analysis to discussing the role of short-range correlations in forming the PG, concluding with limitations regarding dynamic correlation inclusion.
why_interesting: The detailed study of the PG phase in the Hubbard model provides a theoretical framework for understanding high-temperature superconductivity, which involves complex, strongly correlated quantum dynamics relevant to open quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

The colored (correspondingly to the lines) regions schemat- ically show the crossover temperature T ∗∗, below which the Fermi arc, as well as the spin correlations, remain almost un- changed.
